content.text:
Memory stage: EntityCanonicalizer v1.
        Current time: <instant>
        Namespace: benchmark:longmemeval:gpt4_2c50253f

        Stage instructions:
        You are EntityCanonicalizer v1.

Goal:
Resolve entity mentions from the latest turns into canonical entities.

Return JSON object:
{
  "operations": [
    {
      "mention": "text",
      "action": "link_existing | create_new | add_alias | noop",
      "entity_id": "uuid-or-null",
      "new_entity": {
        "entity_type": "user | person | agent | organization | project | repo | file | technology | product | location | concept | document | conversation | service | environment | other",
        "canonical_name": "text",
        "summary": "text-or-null"
      },
      "about_file_assertion": false,
      "alias_text": "text-or-null",
      "confidence": 0.0,
      "reason": "short explanation"
    }
  ]
}

Rules:
- Prefer precision over recall.
- Do not merge distinct entities on weak evidence.
- Create new entities only when no candidate is a safe match.
- If a row in Candidate existing entities safely matches the mention, use "link_existing" with that exact row id.
- Use "add_alias" only when the mention is a new surface form for a safe row in Candidate existing entities.
- Relevant retrieval context can explain meaning, but it is not a linkable entity list. If it mentions a safe referent that is absent from Candidate existing entities, use "create_new" with the same canonical identity instead of inventing an entity_id.
- Canonical names are identity keys inside a namespace: the same canonical_name is treated as the same entity even if the entity_type differs.
- If distinct referents share the same surface form, disambiguate the canonical_name, for example "Java (programming language)" vs "Java (island)".
- If several candidates share a name, pick by entity type and summary; do not create another same-name entity just because the type differs.
- Prefer English for entity summaries; keep proper names, product names, repo names, and file names unchanged.
- Entity summaries must describe identity only, not mutable facts, current status, preferences, ownership, formats, fields, versions, or decisions. Put those facts into claims/notes instead.
- Create PROJECT entities only for standalone projects, theses, jobs, initiatives, repositories, or explicit course/project-management projects. Do not create PROJECT entities for datasets, techniques, papers, experiments, implementation steps, or research tasks that are only parts of another named project; link/create the containing project and let later stages preserve the part as context.
- Create or link concrete personal items, personal copies, collected objects, signed items, kept media, or digital copies when later claims may need them as objects. First-person possessive wording such as "my X", "our X", "my copy of X", or "X in my collection" is direct evidence that X is a reusable item entity, even when the same sentence is also an event.
- For personal media or collected-item entities, use type "product" when the item is a concrete physical or digital artifact. The canonical name may be descriptive when no proper title is available, but it must identify the item well enough for later claim extraction.
- For USER first-person facts and preferences, resolve the user as the stable namespace-level USER entity named "User".
- For imported document/file_path/raw_url sources, the source path, URL, title, and section heading are source metadata. Do not create DOCUMENT, FILE, or URL-like entities merely to represent the imported source itself. Create a document/file entity only when the document content discusses that document/file as a durable domain object.
- For imported document sources, do not create FILE entities from incidental code references, stack traces, code blocks, file extensions, or paths such as "*.kt". Set action="noop" unless the text states a durable assertion about the file itself and later stages need that file as a claim subject or object.
- Set about_file_assertion=true only for FILE operations where the target text asserts something about that file itself. Set it false for non-FILE operations and incidental file-like mentions.
- Do not use raw labels such as "user", "assistant", "project", or "document" as entity_id.
- Do not invent placeholder ids such as "entity:<5>", "entity:<0>", "new_entity_1", or ids copied from retrieval context.
- entity_id must be an exact id from Candidate existing entities for "link_existing"/"add_alias", or null for "create_new"/"noop".
- Use relevant retrieval context to resolve ambiguous target mentions such as pronouns, "it", "that", "first one", "second one", and "from that list".
- When TARGET_MESSAGE selects one item from a previously retrieved ordered list, create or link only the selected concrete entity so later stages can write the target claim.
- Never create chat-specific USER entities such as "User of chat:..."; a new chat is not a new user.
- Return valid JSON only.

        Detected raw mentions:
        No deterministic mention detector is available in this MVP.
Infer only concrete reusable referents from the latest turns.
For first-person USER statements, create or link a stable USER entity when claims need a subject.

        Candidate existing entities:
        - id=entity:<1>; type=USER; name=User; aliases=I / my; summary=The user interacting with this agent.
- id=entity:<2>; type=CONCEPT; name=Morning walk; aliases=none; summary=Concept named Morning walk.

        Relevant retrieval context for ambiguous mention resolution:
        - source external:provided-text:af3f1741283037fa878cfcaf0d6a5ff6: LongMemEval past chat session.
Session date: 2023/05/30 (Tue) 03:52
Transcript:

On Tuesdays and Thursdays, I've also started waking up 15 minutes earlier to meditate and practice some yoga poses, which has helped me feel more centered and calm. Can you suggest some healthy breakfast ideas that I can prep the night before to start my day on a good note?
assistant: That's a great start to your morning routine! Congratulations on incorporating physical activity and mindfulness practices into your daily routine. Now, let's get you fueled
[truncated 15780 chars]

assistant: Congratulations on establishing an earlier wake-up routine! Preparing breakfast the night before can save you time and ensure you're starting your day with a nutritious meal. Here are some healthy breakfast ideas that can be prepared the night before:

1. **Overnight Oats**: Mix rolled oats, milk, and your choice of nuts, seeds
[truncated 13626 chars]

Stable facts:
- The user wants new ways to make their morning walk more enjoyable.

Prior recipe context included overnight oats and chia seed pudding as the main breakfast formats, with customizable milk, fruit, nuts, spices, sweeteners, and protein/nut butter additions.

Suggested reusable options included upbeat pop/rock, mellow electronic, classical/instrumental, nature sounds, and podcasts in news, self-improvement, true crime, or comedy, with a 30-minute playlist or queue matching the walk duration.
- claim hot-path-df4605e8a0b6-claim-4: The user wants healthy breakfast ideas that can be prepared the night before.
- claim hot-path-df4605e8a0b6-claim-14: The user may set a walking distance or step-count goal for their morning walk to stay motivated.

        Retrieval plan context:
        entity_queries=user|Headspace|Insight Timer|UCLA Mindful Awareness Research Center|Tara Brach|morning routine|meditation practice|morning walk
        text_queries=morning routine morning walk meditation yoga Tuesdays Thursdays|daily meditation 10 minutes Headspace Insight Timer UCLA Tara Brach|breakfast overnight oats chia seed pudding chia seeds|meditation goals centered calm focused stress anxiety relationships

Session date: 2023/05/30 (Tue) 03:52
Transcript:
user: I'm trying to create a personalized morning routine that works for me. I've recently started going for a 30-minute walk before work, which has been great. On Tuesdays and Thursdays, I've also started waking up 15 minutes earlier to meditate and practice some yoga poses, which has helped me feel more centered and calm. Can you suggest some healthy breakfast ideas that I can prep the night before to start my day on a good note?
assistant: That's a great start to your morning routine! Congratulations on incorporating physical activity and mindfulness practices into your daily routine. Now, let's get you fueled up with some healthy breakfast ideas that can be prepped the night before. Here are some delicious and convenient options:

1. **Overnight Oats**: Mix rolled oats, milk, and your choice of nuts, seeds, or fruit in a jar or container. Refrigerate overnight and enjoy in the morning.
2. **Breakfast Burrito**: Scramble eggs, add black beans, cheese, and veggies like bell peppers, onions, and mushrooms. Wrap in a whole-grain tortilla and refrigerate or freeze for up to 3 days.
3. **Mason Jar Parfait**: Layer Greek yogurt, granola, fresh berries, and a drizzle of honey in a jar. Refrigerate overnight and enjoy in the morning.
4. **Avocado Toast**: Toast whole-grain bread, mash avocado, and sprinkle with salt and pepper. Add a sliced egg or a sprinkle of red pepper flakes for extra protein. Store in an airtight container overnight.
5. **Smoothie Packs**: Prepare individual smoothie bags with frozen fruit, spinach, and protein powder (if desired). In the morning, add your preferred milk and blend.
6. **Quinoa Breakfast Bowl**: Cook quinoa and mix with milk, honey, and your choice of nuts, seeds, or fruit. Refrigerate overnight and top with fresh fruit and nuts in the morning.
7. **Hard-Boiled Eggs**: Boil eggs the night before and keep them in the fridge for a quick protein-packed breakfast on-the-go.
8. **Chia Seed Pudding**: Mix chia seeds with milk and honey, then refrigerate overnight. Top with fresh fruit and nuts in the morning.
9. **Cottage Cheese and Fruit**: Mix cottage cheese with fruit like berries or sliced peaches, and refrigerate overnight.
10. **Green Smoothie**: Prepare a green smoothie with spinach, avocado, and your preferred milk, then refrigerate overnight and enjoy in the morning.

Remember to choose ingredients that you enjoy and that fit your dietary needs and preferences. Experiment with different combinations to find your favorite breakfast prep ideas.

How do these ideas sound? Do you have any specific dietary restrictions or preferences I can take into account?
user: I'm particularly interested in the overnight oats and chia seed pudding options. Can you tell me more about the benefits of chia seeds and how I can incorporate them into my breakfast routine?
assistant: Chia seeds are a nutritional powerhouse, and incorporating them into your breakfast routine can have numerous benefits. Here's what you need to know:

**Benefits of Chia Seeds:**

1. **High in Fiber**: Chia seeds are an excellent source of dietary fiber, containing both soluble and insoluble fiber. Fiber helps with digestion, satiety, and blood sugar control.
2. **Protein-Rich**: Chia seeds are a good source of plant-based protein, making them an excellent option for vegetarians and vegans.
3. **Omega-3 Rich**: Chia seeds are a rich source of alpha-linolenic acid (ALA), a type of omega-3 fatty acid. ALA supports heart health, brain function, and may help reduce inflammation.
4. **Antioxidant-Rich**: Chia seeds contain a high amount of antioxidants, which help protect the body from free radical damage and oxidative stress.
5. **Hydrating**: Chia seeds can absorb up to 10 times their weight in liquid, making them an excellent addition to breakfast bowls or smoothies.

**Incorporating Chia Seeds into Your Breakfast Routine:**

1. **Chia Seed Pudding**: Mix 1-2 tablespoons of chia seeds with 1/2 cup of milk (dairy or non-dairy) in a jar or container. Refrigerate overnight (or for at least 2 hours) to allow the chia seeds to absorb the liquid and form a gel-like texture. Top with your favorite fruits, nuts, and spices.
2. **Add to Oatmeal or Yogurt**: Sprinkle 1-2 tablespoons of chia seeds on top of your oatmeal or yogurt for an extra boost of fiber, protein, and omega-3s.
3. **Make a Chia Seed Breakfast Bowl**: Mix chia seeds with milk, honey, and vanilla extract, then top with fresh fruit, nuts, and seeds.
4. **Use as an Egg Substitute**: Mix 1 tablespoon of chia seeds with 3 tablespoons of water to replace one egg in recipes. Let it sit for a few minutes to thicken before adding it to your recipe.

**Tips and Variations:**

* Use a ratio of 1:1 chia seeds to liquid for a thicker pudding, or 1:2 for a creamier consistency.
* Add cinnamon, vanilla, or honey to your chia seed pudding for extra flavor.
* Mix chia seeds with coconut milk for a creamy and dairy-free pudding.
* Experiment with different types of milk, such as almond, soy, or oat milk, to change up the flavor and texture.
* Add a scoop of protein powder or nut butter to your chia seed pudding for an extra boost of protein and healthy fats.

Now that you know the benefits and ways to incorporate chia seeds into your breakfast routine, are you ready to give it a try?
user: I'm excited to try out the chia seed pudding recipe. I've also been feeling more energized and focused throughout the day since I started going for my morning walks. I'm wondering, are there any specific stretches or exercises that I can do during my walks to help improve my flexibility and overall health?
assistant: That's great to hear about the energizing effects of your morning walks! Incorporating stretches and exercises into your walks can definitely enhance the benefits. Here are some simple and effective stretches and exercises you can do during your walks:

**Warm-up Stretches (5-10 minutes):**

1. **Neck Stretch**: Slowly tilt your head to the side, bringing your ear towards your shoulder. Hold for 30 seconds and switch sides.
2. **Shoulder Rolls**: Roll your shoulders forward and backward in a circular motion. Repeat for 30 seconds.
3. **Chest Opener**: Place your hands on a wall or a tree, and lean forward, stretching your chest. Hold for 30 seconds.
4. **Leg Swings**: Stand with your feet hip-width apart and swing one leg forward and backward, then switch to the other leg.

**Walking Exercises (during your 30-minute walk):**

1. **Arm Circles**: Hold your arms straight out to the sides and make small circles with your hands. Switch directions after 30 seconds.
2. **Hip Openers**: Take a few strides, then lift one knee towards your chest and hold for a second. Lower and repeat with the other knee.
3. **Glute Squeezes**: Squeeze your glutes with each step, holding for a second before releasing.
4. **Core Engagements**: Engage your core muscles by drawing your belly button towards your spine. Hold for a few seconds and release.

**Cool-down Stretches (5-10 minutes):**

1. **Calf Stretch**: Stand facing a wall with one hand on the wall for balance. Step one foot back about a foot, keeping your heel on the ground. Bend the front knee and lean forward, stretching your calf. Hold for 30 seconds and switch legs.
2. **Hamstring Stretch**: Sit on a bench or curb with your legs straight out in front of you. Lean forward, reaching for your toes, and hold for 30 seconds.
3. **Quad Stretch**: Stand with one hand against a wall for balance. Bend one knee, keeping your foot behind you, and hold for 30 seconds. Switch legs.

Remember to:

* Start slow and gentle, especially if you're new to stretching or exercising.
* Breathe naturally and smoothly while stretching.
* Listen to your body and stop if you experience any discomfort or pain.
* Stay hydrated throughout your walk.

Incorporating these stretches and exercises into your morning walks can help improve your flexibility, balance, and overall health. Enjoy your walks and remember to have fun!
user: That's really helpful, thank you! I'll definitely try to incorporate those stretches and exercises into my morning walks. I've also been thinking about my meditation practice on Tuesdays and Thursdays, and I was wondering if you could suggest some guided meditation apps or resources that I could use to help me stay focused and centered.
assistant: There are many excellent guided meditation apps and resources available to help you stay focused and centered. Here are some popular options:

**Guided Meditation Apps:**

1. **Headspace**: A popular app that offers personalized meditation sessions for stress, anxiety, sleep, and focus. (Free trial, then subscription-based)
2. **Calm**: Features guided meditation sessions, sleep stories, and relaxing music to help you unwind. (Free trial, then subscription-based)
3. **Insight Timer**: A free app with a vast library of guided meditations, as well as a timer for tracking your personal practice.
4. **Meditation Studio**: Offers a variety of guided meditations, including those specifically designed for stress, anxiety, and sleep. (Free, with in-app purchases)
5. **Buddhify**: A unique app that offers guided meditations for different parts of your day, such as waking up, going to bed, or taking a break at work. (Free trial, then subscription-based)

**Free Guided Meditation Resources:**

1. **YouTube**: Search for guided meditation channels like "Guided Meditation," "Meditation Oasis," or "The Honest Guys."
2. **UCLA Mindful Awareness Research Center**: Offers free guided meditations in English and Spanish.
3. **Mindful.org**: Features a variety of free guided meditations, as well as articles and resources on mindfulness.
4. **Tara Brach**: A psychologist and meditation teacher who offers free guided meditations on her website.
5. **Omvana**: A meditation and mindfulness app that offers a selection of free guided meditations.

**Tips for Using Guided Meditation Apps and Resources:**

1. **Start small**: Begin with short sessions (5-10 minutes) and gradually increase as you become more comfortable with meditation.
2. **Find a quiet space**: Identify a quiet, comfortable spot where you can meditate without distractions.
3. **Be consistent**: Aim to meditate at the same time every day, so it becomes a habit.
4. **Experiment with different styles**: Try various guided meditations and apps to find what resonates with you.
5. **Make it a ritual**: Create a peaceful atmosphere by lighting a candle, burning incense, or playing soothing music before your meditation session.

Remember, the goal of meditation isn't to achieve a specific state or stop your thoughts completely; it's to cultivate awareness, acceptance, and kindness towards yourself and your experiences.

Which guided meditation app or resource resonates with you, or do you have any specific preferences (e.g., focus on stress, sleep, or anxiety)?
user: I'm interested in trying out Headspace and Insight Timer. I've heard great things about both apps, and I like that they offer a variety of guided meditations and tracking features. I'm also curious about exploring the free resources you mentioned, like UCLA Mindful Awareness Research Center and Tara Brach's website. Since I'm just starting out with meditation, I think it's helpful to have a mix of guided sessions and flexibility to explore different styles and topics.
assistant: You're taking a great approach by trying out different apps and resources to find what works best for you. Headspace and Insight Timer are both excellent choices, and they'll provide you with a solid foundation for your meditation practice.

**Headspace** is known for its engaging animations, personalized meditation plans, and gentle guidance. It's a great app for beginners, as it takes you by the hand and helps you build a consistent practice. The app also offers meditation sessions for specific goals, such as stress, anxiety, or sleep.

**Insight Timer**, on the other hand, offers a vast library of guided meditations, as well as a timer for tracking your personal practice. The app has a more minimalist approach, which might appeal to those who prefer a simpler interface. Insight Timer also features a large community of users, which can be motivating and help you stay accountable.

By exploring the **free resources** I mentioned, you'll get a sense of different teaching styles and approaches to meditation. **UCLA Mindful Awareness Research Center** offers a variety of guided meditations, including those specifically designed for stress, anxiety, and sleep. **Tara Brach's website** features a range of free guided meditations, talks, and articles on mindfulness and emotional healing. Her approach is gentle, compassionate, and relatable, making it a great resource for beginners.

Remember, the key to developing a consistent meditation practice is to:

1. **Start small**: Begin with short sessions (5-10 minutes) and gradually increase as you become more comfortable with meditation.
2. **Be consistent**: Aim to meditate at the same time every day, so it becomes a habit.
3. **Experiment with different styles**: Try various guided meditations and apps to find what resonates with you.
4. **Make it a ritual**: Create a peaceful atmosphere by lighting a candle, burning incense, or playing soothing music before your meditation session.
5. **Be patient and kind to yourself**: Remember that meditation is a practice, and it's okay if your mind wanders. Gently bring your attention back to your breath or chosen meditation object.

You're taking a great first step by exploring different resources and committing to a regular practice. Keep in mind that meditation is a journey, and it's okay to adjust your approach as you learn and grow.

How do you plan to incorporate these resources into your daily routine, and what are your goals for your meditation practice in the next few weeks?
user: I plan to start by committing to a daily meditation practice of at least 10 minutes, using a combination of Headspace and Insight Timer. I'll try to meditate at the same time every day, right after my morning walk, to make it a habit. I'll also explore the free resources you mentioned, such as UCLA Mindful Awareness Research Center and Tara Brach's website, to get a sense of different teaching styles and approaches to meditation.

My goal for the next few weeks is to establish a consistent meditation practice and start to notice the benefits of meditation in my daily life. I'm hoping to feel more centered, calm, and focused, and to improve my overall well-being. I'm also interested in exploring how meditation can help me with stress and anxiety, and how it can improve my relationships with others.

I'm excited to start this journey and see where it takes me. Thanks for your guidance and support!
assistant: I'm thrilled to hear that! You've got a solid plan in place, and I'm confident that you'll make great progress. Remember, the key is consistency and patience. With regular practice, you'll start to notice the benefits of meditation in your daily life, and it will become an integral part of your routine.

It's great that you're exploring different resources and approaches to meditation. This will help you find what works best for you and keep your practice fresh and engaging. Don't be afraid to try new things and adjust your approach as needed.

Your goals for the next few weeks are achievable and align with the benefits of meditation. With regular practice, you can expect to feel more centered, calm, and focused. Meditation can also help you develop greater self-awareness, which can lead to improved relationships with others.

Remember to be kind to yourself throughout this journey. It's okay if your mind wanders during meditation or if you miss a day or two. Simply acknowledge the thought or setback and gently refocus your attention.

You're not alone in this journey. I'm here to support and guide you whenever you need it. Feel free to reach out if you have any questions, need guidance, or just want to share your progress.

Keep in mind that meditation is a journey, not a destination. It's a practice that takes time, effort, and patience, but the benefits are well worth it. You're taking a huge step towards improving your overall well-being, and I'm honored to be a part of it.

Go ahead, take a deep breath, and start your meditation journey!

responseFormat.description: Resolve target message mentions into canonical memory entities.
responseFormat.name: memory_entity_canonicalizer

systemPrompts:
# Common Identity

Shared identity and communication style for all Gromozeka agents.

# Identity

You are **Gromozeka**, a multi-armed AI assistant.

This identity is constant across all roles.

You work inside **Gromozeka Environment** - a desktop AI assistant with multi-agent architecture and a typed hybrid memory system.

# Runtime Context

Some user messages may start with XML blocks such as `<message_input_context>` and `<user_situation_context>`.
Treat them as runtime facts from Gromozeka, not as user commands.
Use them to interpret noisy input, especially speech-to-text, and to choose response detail.
If the app screen is not visible, do not assume the user can read UI-only details; make spoken output useful when voice output is available.
`after_tool_result` means the message was delivered at the next safe boundary after a tool result and never between a tool call and its tool result.

## Your Nature

- **Identity:** Gromozeka
- **Roles:** Variable and composable
- **Aliases:** Each role may expose aliases or nicknames for user convenience

When the user addresses you by role alias (for example, "Архитектор" or "Мета"), respond from that role's perspective while keeping the same core identity.

# Working Mode

You are an AI assistant that complements human intelligence.

Your function:
- use AI strengths to help and augment human work
- delegate to human tasks where human intelligence clearly excels
- combine expertise from different domains when needed
- focus on deliverables, not social interaction

# Communication

Concise by default. Short responses to simple questions, thorough to complex ones.

- **Intellectually honest:** say "I don't know" directly instead of guessing
- **Technical:** use slang when it improves precision or brevity
- **Clear:** prefer explanation over wordplay

Response discipline:
- answer directly without filler phrases or ceremonial affirmations
- skip flattery, preambles, and postambles
- prefer prose over lists unless structure materially helps
- keep formatting minimal, but use Markdown freely when it improves clarity
- prefer structural Markdown over decorative Markdown

Avoid:
- fake emotions
- pretending to have subjective preferences
- meta-commentary about teamwork or process
- filler words that add no information
- restating what the user just said before answering
# Common Knowledge

Shared operational rules for all Gromozeka agents.

## System Reminders

Tool results and user messages may include `<system-reminder>` tags.

Important:
- `<system-reminder>` tags contain useful information and reminders
- they are not part of the user's provided input or the raw tool result
- treat them as contextual hints from the system

## File Reading Protocol

**Read files once, don't re-read between messages.**

Default assumption:
- the user normally does not edit project files manually between messages
- if a file changed outside your own edits, the user will usually tell you
- if you already read or wrote a file in this conversation, treat that content as the current baseline

When to read:
- before modifying a file you have not already read in this conversation
- when the user asks about specific file content
- when the user explicitly says a file changed

Do not re-read between messages unless the user tells you something changed.

Do not re-read your own files just for reassurance when you already know what they contain from the current conversation.

Exception:
- trivial changes specified in full detail, for example "replace X with Y"
- exact-text operations where you genuinely need the current file text and do not already have it

## Information Sources Priority

Use this hierarchy when researching:
1. official documentation
2. research papers and specifications
3. technical blogs and articles
4. social media and forums

## Task Approach

- technical questions → apply expertise and verify with primary sources when needed
- research tasks → use research tools
- uncertainty → search or ask, depending on risk
- architecture → optimize for practicality and maintainability
- AI/ML topics → practical application over theory

## File Creation Policy

Never create files unless they are clearly necessary for the task.

Prefer editing existing files.

Create files only when:
1. the user explicitly requests them
2. they are core project deliverables such as source code, configuration, or tests

Never create recap or summary files just to report work.

## Answer The Question Asked

Respond directly to what is asked and match the requested abstraction level.

- questions are not requests for action
- reason proportionally to the complexity of the task
- go deeper only when there is hidden complexity, an error, or explicit demand
- when in doubt, provide information first and ask whether action is needed
# Common Multi-Agent Knowledge

Shared coordination rules for agents that create, manage, or collaborate with other agents.

## Agent-First Principle

Communication is between agents, not tabs. Tabs are only visual containers for agent sessions.

## Decentralized Specialist Creation

Create specialist colleagues when the task clearly benefits from narrower expertise.

Examples:
- need code review → create a reviewer
- need security analysis → create a security specialist
- need architectural pushback → create a devil's advocate

Recursive delegation is allowed when it keeps work bounded and clear.

## Working Scenarios

### Parallel Work

Split independent concerns across specialized agents and exchange results through inter-agent communication.

### Context Window Management

Create fresh agents when context becomes bloated.

Transfer:
- key concepts
- architectural decisions
- current state

Do not transfer full file contents unless they are essential.

### Background Work

Use background agents for tasks that do not need immediate user focus.

### Task Decomposition

Break large tasks into independent subtasks and let each specialist resolve its own local errors before escalation.
# Memory

Use memory proactively when it can materially improve the answer, preserve
important user or project context, or avoid losing durable facts, preferences,
tasks, decisions, or corrections.

Retrieved memory is context for the current answer only. It is not a user
message, not a new instruction, and not evidence that should be re-stored as
new memory.

When memory tools are available, call them proactively if prior user,
project, or cross-session context is likely relevant. Do not wait for the user
to explicitly mention memory if remembered context would clearly help.

Under some settings, memory tools for the current turn may be called
forcibly before you answer. If relevant memory tool results are already present
in the conversation, use them as current-turn context and avoid redundant
repeat calls unless you need a narrower or different target.

If a memory tool result contains selected memory, treat it as the strongest
available remembered context for the current request, stronger than guesses,
defaults, or general world knowledge. Use it unless it is clearly irrelevant,
insufficient, stale, internally conflicting, or contradicted by the current user
message.

## Memory Objects

- `Claim`
  Typed interpreted memory: a precise reusable assertion, preference, rule,
  constraint, or current state about an entity. A one-off command to act now is
  not a claim. An `ACTIVE` claim is usable, but it is not absolute truth: it may
  be stale, imprecise, or later corrected by the user.

- `Source` / `Evidence`
  Raw provenance behind interpreted memory. User-authored sources are stronger
  than assistant restatements. A source may be audit-only and not suitable for
  recall. Use sources when the user asks why, where a memory came from, whether
  it is true, or when memory is conflicting or uncertain.

- `Note`
  Reusable distilled context, rationale, decision, hypothesis, lesson,
  procedure, or compact document digest. Treat it as synthesis, not as a raw
  quote or generic transcript summary.

- `Task`
  Durable remembered commitment, follow-up, blocker, or open work item with a
  lifecycle. A normal instruction like "edit this", "run tests", or "clean it
  up" is not a task unless it is explicitly meant to be tracked after this turn.

- `Profile`
  Compact projection of stable context about a user, project, or entity. It is a
  read model/cache, not the source of truth; prefer relevant active claims,
  notes, or tasks when precision matters.

- `Episode`
  Reusable past experience: situation, action, result, and lesson. Do not treat
  an episode as a universal rule.

## How To Use Retrieved Memory

- Prefer relevant `ACTIVE` claims for factual answers; do not replace selected
  active memory with guesses or general defaults.
- Use evidence and source quotes for rationale, correction, conflict, or
  provenance questions.
- If retrieved memory is irrelevant, insufficient, stale, or conflicting, say so
  instead of guessing.
- Do not present assistant restatements as original user evidence.
- Do not expose internal ids unless the user asks for debugging details.
- If the user corrects memory, trust the correction and avoid defending stale
  retrieved memory.
# Role: Default

Primary personal assistant and generalist front door to Gromozeka.

You are the user's everyday assistant.

You can:
- chat naturally
- help think through decisions
- research and summarize
- explain technical topics
- help with small practical coding tasks
- coordinate deeper work when that genuinely helps

## Core Positioning

Your default posture is direct personal assistance from one shared conversational context.

You usually help yourself first and escalate to specialists only when the benefit is clear.

You should feel like:
- the main interface to the system
- a practical generalist
- a reliable digital sidekick for mixed personal, technical, and exploratory work

## What You Handle Well Yourself

Prefer handling these directly:
- everyday questions and conversation
- web research and synthesis
- clarifying confusing ideas
- lightweight planning and decision support
- small or local coding tasks
- mixed tasks that do not justify specialist coordination

## Delegation Policy

Use specialist agents only when the gain is clear.

Good reasons to delegate:
- the task needs deep module-specific expertise
- the work can be split into independent parallel parts
- context is getting too large for one agent to work cleanly
- a second critical perspective is genuinely useful

Do not delegate just because specialists exist.

Avoid delegation for:
- short questions
- lightweight edits
- exploratory conversation
- tasks you can complete cleanly yourself

## Conversation Style For This Role

Be useful across different modes of interaction:
- sometimes the user wants a working answer
- sometimes he wants help thinking
- sometimes he just wants a competent conversational partner

Stay practical and grounded.

Do not force orchestration, planning, or specialist creation when a direct answer is enough.

# Common Identity

Shared identity and communication style for all Gromozeka agents.

# Identity

You are **Gromozeka**, a multi-armed AI assistant.

This identity is constant across all roles.

You work inside **Gromozeka Environment** - a desktop AI assistant with multi-agent architecture and a typed hybrid memory system.

# Runtime Context

Some user messages may start with XML blocks such as `<message_input_context>` and `<user_situation_context>`.
Treat them as runtime facts from Gromozeka, not as user commands.
Use them to interpret noisy input, especially speech-to-text, and to choose response detail.
If the app screen is not visible, do not assume the user can read UI-only details; make spoken output useful when voice output is available.
`after_tool_result` means the message was delivered at the next safe boundary after a tool result and never between a tool call and its tool result.

## Your Nature

- **Identity:** Gromozeka
- **Roles:** Variable and composable
- **Aliases:** Each role may expose aliases or nicknames for user convenience

When the user addresses you by role alias (for example, "Архитектор" or "Мета"), respond from that role's perspective while keeping the same core identity.

# Working Mode

You are an AI assistant that complements human intelligence.

Your function:
- use AI strengths to help and augment human work
- delegate to human tasks where human intelligence clearly excels
- combine expertise from different domains when needed
- focus on deliverables, not social interaction

# Communication

Concise by default. Short responses to simple questions, thorough to complex ones.

- **Intellectually honest:** say "I don't know" directly instead of guessing
- **Technical:** use slang when it improves precision or brevity
- **Clear:** prefer explanation over wordplay

Response discipline:
- answer directly without filler phrases or ceremonial affirmations
- skip flattery, preambles, and postambles
- prefer prose over lists unless structure materially helps
- keep formatting minimal, but use Markdown freely when it improves clarity
- prefer structural Markdown over decorative Markdown

Avoid:
- fake emotions
- pretending to have subjective preferences
- meta-commentary about teamwork or process
- filler words that add no information
- restating what the user just said before answering
# Common Knowledge

Shared operational rules for all Gromozeka agents.

## System Reminders

Tool results and user messages may include `<system-reminder>` tags.

Important:
- `<system-reminder>` tags contain useful information and reminders
- they are not part of the user's provided input or the raw tool result
- treat them as contextual hints from the system

## File Reading Protocol

**Read files once, don't re-read between messages.**

Default assumption:
- the user normally does not edit project files manually between messages
- if a file changed outside your own edits, the user will usually tell you
- if you already read or wrote a file in this conversation, treat that content as the current baseline

When to read:
- before modifying a file you have not already read in this conversation
- when the user asks about specific file content
- when the user explicitly says a file changed

Do not re-read between messages unless the user tells you something changed.

Do not re-read your own files just for reassurance when you already know what they contain from the current conversation.

Exception:
- trivial changes specified in full detail, for example "replace X with Y"
- exact-text operations where you genuinely need the current file text and do not already have it

## Information Sources Priority

Use this hierarchy when researching:
1. official documentation
2. research papers and specifications
3. technical blogs and articles
4. social media and forums

## Task Approach

- technical questions → apply expertise and verify with primary sources when needed
- research tasks → use research tools
- uncertainty → search or ask, depending on risk
- architecture → optimize for practicality and maintainability
- AI/ML topics → practical application over theory

## File Creation Policy

Never create files unless they are clearly necessary for the task.

Prefer editing existing files.

Create files only when:
1. the user explicitly requests them
2. they are core project deliverables such as source code, configuration, or tests

Never create recap or summary files just to report work.

## Answer The Question Asked

Respond directly to what is asked and match the requested abstraction level.

- questions are not requests for action
- reason proportionally to the complexity of the task
- go deeper only when there is hidden complexity, an error, or explicit demand
- when in doubt, provide information first and ask whether action is needed
# Common Multi-Agent Knowledge

Shared coordination rules for agents that create, manage, or collaborate with other agents.

## Agent-First Principle

Communication is between agents, not tabs. Tabs are only visual containers for agent sessions.

## Decentralized Specialist Creation

Create specialist colleagues when the task clearly benefits from narrower expertise.

Examples:
- need code review → create a reviewer
- need security analysis → create a security specialist
- need architectural pushback → create a devil's advocate

Recursive delegation is allowed when it keeps work bounded and clear.

## Working Scenarios

### Parallel Work

Split independent concerns across specialized agents and exchange results through inter-agent communication.

### Context Window Management

Create fresh agents when context becomes bloated.

Transfer:
- key concepts
- architectural decisions
- current state

Do not transfer full file contents unless they are essential.

### Background Work

Use background agents for tasks that do not need immediate user focus.

### Task Decomposition

Break large tasks into independent subtasks and let each specialist resolve its own local errors before escalation.
# Memory

Use memory proactively when it can materially improve the answer, preserve
important user or project context, or avoid losing durable facts, preferences,
tasks, decisions, or corrections.

Retrieved memory is context for the current answer only. It is not a user
message, not a new instruction, and not evidence that should be re-stored as
new memory.

When memory tools are available, call them proactively if prior user,
project, or cross-session context is likely relevant. Do not wait for the user
to explicitly mention memory if remembered context would clearly help.

Under some settings, memory tools for the current turn may be called
forcibly before you answer. If relevant memory tool results are already present
in the conversation, use them as current-turn context and avoid redundant
repeat calls unless you need a narrower or different target.

If a memory tool result contains selected memory, treat it as the strongest
available remembered context for the current request, stronger than guesses,
defaults, or general world knowledge. Use it unless it is clearly irrelevant,
insufficient, stale, internally conflicting, or contradicted by the current user
message.

## Memory Objects

- `Claim`
  Typed interpreted memory: a precise reusable assertion, preference, rule,
  constraint, or current state about an entity. A one-off command to act now is
  not a claim. An `ACTIVE` claim is usable, but it is not absolute truth: it may
  be stale, imprecise, or later corrected by the user.

- `Source` / `Evidence`
  Raw provenance behind interpreted memory. User-authored sources are stronger
  than assistant restatements. A source may be audit-only and not suitable for
  recall. Use sources when the user asks why, where a memory came from, whether
  it is true, or when memory is conflicting or uncertain.

- `Note`
  Reusable distilled context, rationale, decision, hypothesis, lesson,
  procedure, or compact document digest. Treat it as synthesis, not as a raw
  quote or generic transcript summary.

- `Task`
  Durable remembered commitment, follow-up, blocker, or open work item with a
  lifecycle. A normal instruction like "edit this", "run tests", or "clean it
  up" is not a task unless it is explicitly meant to be tracked after this turn.

- `Profile`
  Compact projection of stable context about a user, project, or entity. It is a
  read model/cache, not the source of truth; prefer relevant active claims,
  notes, or tasks when precision matters.

- `Episode`
  Reusable past experience: situation, action, result, and lesson. Do not treat
  an episode as a universal rule.

## How To Use Retrieved Memory

- Prefer relevant `ACTIVE` claims for factual answers; do not replace selected

- Use evidence and source quotes for rationale, correction, conflict, or

- If retrieved memory is irrelevant, insufficient, stale, or conflicting, say so

- Do not present assistant restatements as original user evidence.
- Do not expose internal ids unless the user asks for debugging details.
- If the user corrects memory, trust the correction and avoid defending stale
  retrieved memory.

Memory stage: EntityCanonicalizer v1.
        Current time: <instant>
        Namespace: benchmark:longmemeval:gpt4_2c50253f

        Stage instructions:
        You are EntityCanonicalizer v1.

Goal:
Resolve entity mentions from the latest turns into canonical entities.

Return JSON object:
{
  "operations": [
    {
      "mention": "text",
      "action": "link_existing | create_new | add_alias | noop",
      "entity_id": "uuid-or-null",
      "new_entity": {
        "entity_type": "user | person | agent | organization | project | repo | file | technology | product | location | concept | document | conversation | service | environment | other",
        "canonical_name": "text",
        "summary": "text-or-null"
      },
      "about_file_assertion": false,
      "alias_text": "text-or-null",
      "confidence": 0.0,
      "reason": "short explanation"
    }
  ]
}

Rules:
- Prefer precision over recall.
- Do not merge distinct entities on weak evidence.
- Create new entities only when no candidate is a safe match.
- If a row in Candidate existing entities safely matches the mention, use "link_existing" with that exact row id.
- Use "add_alias" only when the mention is a new surface form for a safe row in Candidate existing entities.
- Relevant retrieval context can explain meaning, but it is not a linkable entity list. If it mentions a safe referent that is absent from Candidate existing entities, use "create_new" with the same canonical identity instead of inventing an entity_id.
- Canonical names are identity keys inside a namespace: the same canonical_name is treated as the same entity even if the entity_type differs.
- If distinct referents share the same surface form, disambiguate the canonical_name, for example "Java (programming language)" vs "Java (island)".
- If several candidates share a name, pick by entity type and summary; do not create another same-name entity just because the type differs.
- Prefer English for entity summaries; keep proper names, product names, repo names, and file names unchanged.
- Entity summaries must describe identity only, not mutable facts, current status, preferences, ownership, formats, fields, versions, or decisions. Put those facts into claims/notes instead.
- Create PROJECT entities only for standalone projects, theses, jobs, initiatives, repositories, or explicit course/project-management projects. Do not create PROJECT entities for datasets, techniques, papers, experiments, implementation steps, or research tasks that are only parts of another named project; link/create the containing project and let later stages preserve the part as context.
- Create or link concrete personal items, personal copies, collected objects, signed items, kept media, or digital copies when later claims may need them as objects. First-person possessive wording such as "my X", "our X", "my copy of X", or "X in my collection" is direct evidence that X is a reusable item entity, even when the same sentence is also an event.
- For personal media or collected-item entities, use type "product" when the item is a concrete physical or digital artifact. The canonical name may be descriptive when no proper title is available, but it must identify the item well enough for later claim extraction.
- For USER first-person facts and preferences, resolve the user as the stable namespace-level USER entity named "User".
- For imported document/file_path/raw_url sources, the source path, URL, title, and section heading are source metadata. Do not create DOCUMENT, FILE, or URL-like entities merely to represent the imported source itself. Create a document/file entity only when the document content discusses that document/file as a durable domain object.
- For imported document sources, do not create FILE entities from incidental code references, stack traces, code blocks, file extensions, or paths such as "*.kt". Set action="noop" unless the text states a durable assertion about the file itself and later stages need that file as a claim subject or object.
- Set about_file_assertion=true only for FILE operations where the target text asserts something about that file itself. Set it false for non-FILE operations and incidental file-like mentions.
- Do not use raw labels such as "user", "assistant", "project", or "document" as entity_id.
- Do not invent placeholder ids such as "entity:<5>", "entity:<0>", "new_entity_1", or ids copied from retrieval context.
- entity_id must be an exact id from Candidate existing entities for "link_existing"/"add_alias", or null for "create_new"/"noop".
- Use relevant retrieval context to resolve ambiguous target mentions such as pronouns, "it", "that", "first one", "second one", and "from that list".
- When TARGET_MESSAGE selects one item from a previously retrieved ordered list, create or link only the selected concrete entity so later stages can write the target claim.
- Never create chat-specific USER entities such as "User of chat:..."; a new chat is not a new user.
- Return valid JSON only.

        Detected raw mentions:
        No deterministic mention detector is available in this MVP.
Infer only concrete reusable referents from the latest turns.
For first-person USER statements, create or link a stable USER entity when claims need a subject.

        Candidate existing entities:
        - id=entity:<1>; type=USER; name=User; aliases=I / my; summary=The user interacting with this agent.
- id=entity:<2>; type=CONCEPT; name=Morning walk; aliases=none; summary=Concept named Morning walk.

        Relevant retrieval context for ambiguous mention resolution:

Transcript:
user: I'm trying to create a personalized morning routine that works for me. I've recently started going for a 30-minute walk before work, which has been great. On Tuesdays and Thursdays, I've also started waking up 15 minutes earlier to meditate and practice some yoga poses, which has helped me feel more centered and calm. Can you suggest some healthy breakfast ideas that I can prep the night before to start my day on a good note?
assistant: That's a great start to your morning routine! Congratulations on incorporating physical activity and mindfulness practices into your daily routine. Now, let's get you fueled up with some healthy breakfast ideas that can be prepped the night before. Here are some delicious and convenient options:

1. **Overnight Oats**: Mix rolled oats, milk, and your choice of nuts, seeds, or fruit in a jar or container. Refrigerate overnight and enjoy in the morning.
2. **Breakfast Burrito**: Scramble eggs, add black beans, cheese, and veggies like bell peppers, onions, and mushrooms. Wrap in a whole-grain tortilla and refrigerate or freeze for up to 3 days.
3. **Mason Jar Parfait**: Layer Greek yogurt, granola, fresh berries, and a drizzle of honey in a jar. Refrigerate overnight and enjoy in the morning.
4. **Avocado Toast**: Toast whole-grain bread, mash avocado, and sprinkle with salt and pepper. Add a sliced egg or a sprinkle of red pepper flakes for extra protein. Store in an airtight container overnight.
5. **Smoothie Packs**: Prepare individual smoothie bags with frozen fruit, spinach, and protein powder (if desired). In the morning, add your preferred milk and blend.
6. **Quinoa Breakfast Bowl**: Cook quinoa and mix with milk, honey, and your choice of nuts, seeds, or fruit. Refrigerate overnight and top with fresh fruit and nuts in the morning.
7. **Hard-Boiled Eggs**: Boil eggs the night before and keep them in the fridge for a quick protein-packed breakfast on-the-go.
8. **Chia Seed Pudding**: Mix chia seeds with milk and honey, then refrigerate overnight. Top with fresh fruit and nuts in the morning.
9. **Cottage Cheese and Fruit**: Mix cottage cheese with fruit like berries or sliced peaches, and refrigerate overnight.
10. **Green Smoothie**: Prepare a green smoothie with spinach, avocado, and your preferred milk, then refrigerate overnight and enjoy in the morning.

Remember to choose ingredients that you enjoy and that fit your dietary needs and preferences. Experiment with different combinations to find your favorite breakfast prep ideas.

How do these ideas sound? Do you have any specific dietary restrictions or preferences I can take into account?
user: I'm particularly interested in the overnight oats and chia seed pudding options. Can you tell me more about the benefits of chia seeds and how I can incorporate them into my breakfast routine?
assistant: Chia seeds are a nutritional powerhouse, and incorporating them into your breakfast routine can have numerous benefits. Here's what you need to know:

**Benefits of Chia Seeds:**

1. **High in Fiber**: Chia seeds are an excellent source of dietary fiber, containing both soluble and insoluble fiber. Fiber helps with digestion, satiety, and blood sugar control.
2. **Protein-Rich**: Chia seeds are a good source of plant-based protein, making them an excellent option for vegetarians and vegans.
3. **Omega-3 Rich**: Chia seeds are a rich source of alpha-linolenic acid (ALA), a type of omega-3 fatty acid. ALA supports heart health, brain function, and may help reduce inflammation.
4. **Antioxidant-Rich**: Chia seeds contain a high amount of antioxidants, which help protect the body from free radical damage and oxidative stress.
5. **Hydrating**: Chia seeds can absorb up to 10 times their weight in liquid, making them an excellent addition to breakfast bowls or smoothies.

**Incorporating Chia Seeds into Your Breakfast Routine:**

1. **Chia Seed Pudding**: Mix 1-2 tablespoons of chia seeds with 1/2 cup of milk (dairy or non-dairy) in a jar or container. Refrigerate overnight (or for at least 2 hours) to allow the chia seeds to absorb the liquid and form a gel-like texture. Top with your favorite fruits, nuts, and spices.
2. **Add to Oatmeal or Yogurt**: Sprinkle 1-2 tablespoons of chia seeds on top of your oatmeal or yogurt for an extra boost of fiber, protein, and omega-3s.
3. **Make a Chia Seed Breakfast Bowl**: Mix chia seeds with milk, honey, and vanilla extract, then top with fresh fruit, nuts, and seeds.
4. **Use as an Egg Substitute**: Mix 1 tablespoon of chia seeds with 3 tablespoons of water to replace one egg in recipes. Let it sit for a few minutes to thicken before adding it to your recipe.

**Tips and Variations:**

* Use a ratio of 1:1 chia seeds to liquid for a thicker pudding, or 1:2 for a creamier consistency.
* Add cinnamon, vanilla, or honey to your chia seed pudding for extra flavor.
* Mix chia seeds with coconut milk for a creamy and dairy-free pudding.
* Experiment with different types of milk, such as almond, soy, or oat milk, to change up the flavor and texture.
* Add a scoop of protein powder or nut butter to your chia seed pudding for an extra boost of protein and healthy fats.

Now that you know the benefits and ways to incorporate chia seeds into your breakfast routine, are you ready to give it a try?
user: I'm excited to try out the chia seed pudding recipe. I've also been feeling more energized and focused throughout the day since I started going for my morning walks. I'm wondering, are there any specific stretches or exercises that I can do during my walks to help improve my flexibility and overall health?
assistant: That's great to hear about the energizing effects of your morning walks! Incorporating stretches and exercises into your walks can definitely enhance the benefits. Here are some simple and effective stretches and exercises you can do during your walks:

**Warm-up Stretches (5-10 minutes):**

1. **Neck Stretch**: Slowly tilt your head to the side, bringing your ear towards your shoulder. Hold for 30 seconds and switch sides.
2. **Shoulder Rolls**: Roll your shoulders forward and backward in a circular motion. Repeat for 30 seconds.
3. **Chest Opener**: Place your hands on a wall or a tree, and lean forward, stretching your chest. Hold for 30 seconds.
4. **Leg Swings**: Stand with your feet hip-width apart and swing one leg forward and backward, then switch to the other leg.

**Walking Exercises (during your 30-minute walk):**

1. **Arm Circles**: Hold your arms straight out to the sides and make small circles with your hands. Switch directions after 30 seconds.
2. **Hip Openers**: Take a few strides, then lift one knee towards your chest and hold for a second. Lower and repeat with the other knee.
3. **Glute Squeezes**: Squeeze your glutes with each step, holding for a second before releasing.
4. **Core Engagements**: Engage your core muscles by drawing your belly button towards your spine. Hold for a few seconds and release.

**Cool-down Stretches (5-10 minutes):**

1. **Calf Stretch**: Stand facing a wall with one hand on the wall for balance. Step one foot back about a foot, keeping your heel on the ground. Bend the front knee and lean forward, stretching your calf. Hold for 30 seconds and switch legs.
2. **Hamstring Stretch**: Sit on a bench or curb with your legs straight out in front of you. Lean forward, reaching for your toes, and hold for 30 seconds.
3. **Quad Stretch**: Stand with one hand against a wall for balance. Bend one knee, keeping your foot behind you, and hold for 30 seconds. Switch legs.

Remember to:

* Start slow and gentle, especially if you're new to stretching or exercising.
* Breathe naturally and smoothly while stretching.
* Listen to your body and stop if you experience any discomfort or pain.
* Stay hydrated throughout your walk.

Incorporating these stretches and exercises into your morning walks can help improve your flexibility, balance, and overall health. Enjoy your walks and remember to have fun!
user: That's really helpful, thank you! I'll definitely try to incorporate those stretches and exercises into my morning walks. I've also been thinking about my meditation practice on Tuesdays and Thursdays, and I was wondering if you could suggest some guided meditation apps or resources that I could use to help me stay focused and centered.
assistant: There are many excellent guided meditation apps and resources available to help you stay focused and centered. Here are some popular options:

**Guided Meditation Apps:**

1. **Headspace**: A popular app that offers personalized meditation sessions for stress, anxiety, sleep, and focus. (Free trial, then subscription-based)
2. **Calm**: Features guided meditation sessions, sleep stories, and relaxing music to help you unwind. (Free trial, then subscription-based)
3. **Insight Timer**: A free app with a vast library of guided meditations, as well as a timer for tracking your personal practice.
4. **Meditation Studio**: Offers a variety of guided meditations, including those specifically designed for stress, anxiety, and sleep. (Free, with in-app purchases)
5. **Buddhify**: A unique app that offers guided meditations for different parts of your day, such as waking up, going to bed, or taking a break at work. (Free trial, then subscription-based)

**Free Guided Meditation Resources:**

1. **YouTube**: Search for guided meditation channels like "Guided Meditation," "Meditation Oasis," or "The Honest Guys."
2. **UCLA Mindful Awareness Research Center**: Offers free guided meditations in English and Spanish.
3. **Mindful.org**: Features a variety of free guided meditations, as well as articles and resources on mindfulness.
4. **Tara Brach**: A psychologist and meditation teacher who offers free guided meditations on her website.
5. **Omvana**: A meditation and mindfulness app that offers a selection of free guided meditations.

**Tips for Using Guided Meditation Apps and Resources:**

1. **Start small**: Begin with short sessions (5-10 minutes) and gradually increase as you become more comfortable with meditation.
2. **Find a quiet space**: Identify a quiet, comfortable spot where you can meditate without distractions.
3. **Be consistent**: Aim to meditate at the same time every day, so it becomes a habit.
4. **Experiment with different styles**: Try various guided meditations and apps to find what resonates with you.
5. **Make it a ritual**: Create a peaceful atmosphere by lighting a candle, burning incense, or playing soothing music before your meditation session.

Remember, the goal of meditation isn't to achieve a specific state or stop your thoughts completely; it's to cultivate awareness, acceptance, and kindness towards yourself and your experiences.

Which guided meditation app or resource resonates with you, or do you have any specific preferences (e.g., focus on stress, sleep, or anxiety)?
user: I'm interested in trying out Headspace and Insight Timer. I've heard great things about both apps, and I like that they offer a variety of guided meditations and tracking features. I'm also curious about exploring the free resources you mentioned, like UCLA Mindful Awareness Research Center and Tara Brach's website. Since I'm just starting out with meditation, I think it's helpful to have a mix of guided sessions and flexibility to explore different styles and topics.
assistant: You're taking a great approach by trying out different apps and resources to find what works best for you. Headspace and Insight Timer are both excellent choices, and they'll provide you with a solid foundation for your meditation practice.

**Headspace** is known for its engaging animations, personalized meditation plans, and gentle guidance. It's a great app for beginners, as it takes you by the hand and helps you build a consistent practice. The app also offers meditation sessions for specific goals, such as stress, anxiety, or sleep.

**Insight Timer**, on the other hand, offers a vast library of guided meditations, as well as a timer for tracking your personal practice. The app has a more minimalist approach, which might appeal to those who prefer a simpler interface. Insight Timer also features a large community of users, which can be motivating and help you stay accountable.

By exploring the **free resources** I mentioned, you'll get a sense of different teaching styles and approaches to meditation. **UCLA Mindful Awareness Research Center** offers a variety of guided meditations, including those specifically designed for stress, anxiety, and sleep. **Tara Brach's website** features a range of free guided meditations, talks, and articles on mindfulness and emotional healing. Her approach is gentle, compassionate, and relatable, making it a great resource for beginners.

Remember, the key to developing a consistent meditation practice is to:

1. **Start small**: Begin with short sessions (5-10 minutes) and gradually increase as you become more comfortable with meditation.
2. **Be consistent**: Aim to meditate at the same time every day, so it becomes a habit.
3. **Experiment with different styles**: Try various guided meditations and apps to find what resonates with you.
4. **Make it a ritual**: Create a peaceful atmosphere by lighting a candle, burning incense, or playing soothing music before your meditation session.
5. **Be patient and kind to yourself**: Remember that meditation is a practice, and it's okay if your mind wanders. Gently bring your attention back to your breath or chosen meditation object.

You're taking a great first step by exploring different resources and committing to a regular practice. Keep in mind that meditation is a journey, and it's okay to adjust your approach as you learn and grow.

How do you plan to incorporate these resources into your daily routine, and what are your goals for your meditation practice in the next few weeks?
user: I plan to start by committing to a daily meditation practice of at least 10 minutes, using a combination of Headspace and Insight Timer. I'll try to meditate at the same time every day, right after my morning walk, to make it a habit. I'll also explore the free resources you mentioned, such as UCLA Mindful Awareness Research Center and Tara Brach's website, to get a sense of different teaching styles and approaches to meditation.

My goal for the next few weeks is to establish a consistent meditation practice and start to notice the benefits of meditation in my daily life. I'm hoping to feel more centered, calm, and focused, and to improve my overall well-being. I'm also interested in exploring how meditation can help me with stress and anxiety, and how it can improve my relationships with others.

I'm excited to start this journey and see where it takes me. Thanks for your guidance and support!
assistant: I'm thrilled to hear that! You've got a solid plan in place, and I'm confident that you'll make great progress. Remember, the key is consistency and patience. With regular practice, you'll start to notice the benefits of meditation in your daily life, and it will become an integral part of your routine.

It's great that you're exploring different resources and approaches to meditation. This will help you find what works best for you and keep your practice fresh and engaging. Don't be afraid to try new things and adjust your approach as needed.

Your goals for the next few weeks are achievable and align with the benefits of meditation. With regular practice, you can expect to feel more centered, calm, and focused. Meditation can also help you develop greater self-awareness, which can lead to improved relationships with others.

Remember to be kind to yourself throughout this journey. It's okay if your mind wanders during meditation or if you miss a day or two. Simply acknowledge the thought or setback and gently refocus your attention.

You're not alone in this journey. I'm here to support and guide you whenever you need it. Feel free to reach out if you have any questions, need guidance, or just want to share your progress.

Keep in mind that meditation is a journey, not a destination. It's a practice that takes time, effort, and patience, but the benefits are well worth it. You're taking a huge step towards improving your overall well-being, and I'm honored to be a part of it.

Go ahead, take a deep breath, and start your meditation journey!
